description: Scatter plot comparing specific energy absorption values of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels against conventional deep-drawing steels as a function of aluminum content. The y-axis displays specific energy absorption in J/mm³ ranging from 0.0 to 0.6, while the x-axis shows Al content in weight percent from 6 to 12. Conventional deep-drawing steels—including IF steel, HC300LA, and DC04, represented by distinct square symbols at approximately 6 wt% Al—exhibit specific energy absorption values between 0.16 and 0.25 J/mm³. In contrast, Fe-Mn-Al-C steels, depicted as green diamonds tested at deformation rates of 10³/s and 10⁴/s, demonstrate significantly higher energy absorption capacity, clustering around 0.5 J/mm³ when Al content exceeds 7 wt%, with individual data points ranging from approximately 0.43 to 0.55 J/mm³. The figure illustrates that Fe-Mn-Al-C steels achieve approximately 50% higher specific energy absorption than conventional deep-drawing steels at crash-relevant strain rates, a performance level comparable to Mn-TWIP steels. This enhancement is attributed to severe adiabatic shear band formation during high-strain-rate deformation, as evidenced by the elevated energy absorption values at Al contents above 7 wt%, highlighting the suitability of these alloys for automotive body and frame structures requiring superior crashworthiness.
